data.title: L3Harris tests autonomous electronic warfare system in U.S. Army exercise
data.description:
L3Harris Technologies (LHX) on Tuesday said it recently demonstrated an autonomous electronic warfare capability during a U.S. Army experiment, highlighting advances in systems designed to detect and disrupt threats with limited human input.

The test involved the company’s Deceptor electronic warfare payload, which was mounted on multiple unmanned aerial systems. Operating within a broader networked environment, the system was used to identify and track radio frequency signals, combine data from different sensors and carry out jamming operations against identified threats.

According to the company, the system is designed to operate across distributed platforms, enabling coordination between multiple unmanned assets in real time. The demonstration also included the ability to geolocate signals and share targeting data across the network.

Electronic warfare, which focuses on controlling and exploiting the electromagnetic spectrum, has become an increasing area of focus for military planners as modern conflicts rely more heavily on communications and sensor networks.

L3Harris (LHX) said the payload uses commercially available hardware and a modular design, allowing it to be integrated across a range of air, ground and maritime systems. The company indicated that its approach is aimed at enabling deployment across large numbers of smaller platforms rather than relying solely on traditional, centralized systems.

The demonstration builds on ongoing development of compact electronic warfare technologies, with additional tests planned to further integrate the system into multi-domain operations.

data.title: Pentagon awards Lockheed Martin up to $1.9B contract for C-130J training systems
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Defense awarded Lockheed Martin (LMT) a contract worth up to $1.9 billion to continue providing training systems and support for the C-130J military transport aircraft, according to a company release.

The agreement is structured as a 10-year, sole-source, indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity contract, allowing the Pentagon to order services as needed over the life of the program.

The contract covers the next phase of the C-130J Maintenance and Aircrew Training System program, which provides simulators, instructional materials and logistical support for pilots and maintenance personnel. Work will be carried out for multiple U.S. military branches, coordinated through the Air Force Life Cycle Management Center.

The program currently supports units across the Air Force, Air National Guard, Air Force Reserve, Marine Corps and Air Force Special Operations Command, among others. Under the new contract, training support will be expanded to include the Navy Reserve and the Coast Guard.

The C-130J is a modernized version of the long-running Hercules transport aircraft, widely used for tactical airlift missions. The platform is operated by dozens of countries, and the global fleet has accumulated millions of flight hours.

Indefinite delivery contracts such as this one are commonly used by the Pentagon for long-term service and support programs, enabling flexibility in procurement while maintaining continuity for established systems.

data.title: Omada Health wins New Outperform at Citizens on risk-reward setup
data.description:
Omada Health (OMDA) traded higher on Tuesday after Citizens launched its coverage with a Market Outperform rating and an $18 per share target, citing a compelling risk-reward profile for the virtual care provider.

Analyst Constantine Davides argued that Omada (OMDA), unlike its rivals, operates a diversified platform offering solutions for weight loss, diabetes, etc., and its market reach has now exceeded 2,000 customer relationships.

Having traded at an average EV/NTM revenue multiple of 3.5x since its IPO in June 2025, Omada (OMDA) recently traded at 1.7x at Citizens’ 2026 revenue estimate, according to Davides.

Noting that this year OMDA’s membership could surpass 1M, Davides projects 16% and 44% CAGR for the company's revenue and EBITDA over the next four years, respectively.

“Active members on the platform swelled 55% in 2025 to nearly 900K individuals but still only represent ~4% of the 25M lives that have access to OMDA's solutions, so we believe ample runway exists to sustain its double-digit growth rate over the next several years,” Davides wrote.

data.title: Versigent rallies after UBS points to attractive valuation, dividend potential
data.description:
Versigent PLC (VGNT) traded higher on Tuesday after UBS started off coverage on the stock with a Buy rating.

Analyst Joseph Spak said the firm's bullish view on the new company that was spun off from Aptiv (APTV) is centered on the current implied trading EV/EBITDA multiple being too low in comparison to other auto industry suppliers. "We see the stock re-rating as long as management commits to a consistent capital return program. We believe a potential buyback authorization and/or dividend initiation are likely positive catalysts, and we believe that ~50% of the current market cap could be returned to shareholders over the coming three years," highlighted Spak.

UBS assigned a price target of $43 to Versigent (VGNT) based on a 4.5X multiple to the 2027 EV/EBITDA estimate.

Shares of Versigent (VGNT) were up 5.9% in Tuesday afternoon trading to $32.33 vs. the post-spinoff range of $26.34 to $34.99.

data.title: HubSpot's international and partner growth set to continue in 2026: Needham
data.description:
Needham said HubSpot's (HUBS) international and partner growth trajectory is set to continue in 2026 while highlighting several update items for the company's business.

The firm kept its Buy rating and $300 price target on HubSpot's stock. Shares of the company climbed about 1% on Tuesday.

Analysts led by Joshua Reilly said that on the pricing and sales and marketing front, self-service is now launched, which is part of the multi-year sales and marketing, or S&M, efficiency story. The analysts added that outcome versus usage pricing is now the plan moving forward for Breeze Agents, which they expect to drive increased usage.

Breeze Agents are AI-powered agents by HubSpot designed to automate complex, multi-step marketing, sales, and service workflows.

"Additionally, we highlight trends around international and partner sales outperformance in 2025, and our view that these trends continue and possibly accelerate in 2026 as international momentum is driven by larger multihub deals and strength in EMEA [Europe, the Middle East, and Africa], while partner activity is benefiting from AI complexity, international bias towards partner channel and overall up-market shift for the company, which drives greater partner activity," said Reilly and his team.

The analysts noted that the company recently began disclosing how many of their customers are using their Breeze Agents, more specifically the Customer, Prospecting, and Data Agents. In addition, Reilly and his team said that Breeze Assistant usage is growing as expected.

data.title: Autodesk, Datadog and Zscaler are Macquarie's favorite software stocks ahead of earnings
data.description:
Despite concerns about artificial intelligence and its impact on the enterprise software market, Macquarie said it likes Autodesk (ADSK), Datadog (DDOG), and Zscaler (ZS) going into earnings season. 

“We recommend investors buy ADSK based on several potential catalysts, including increasing penetration in the greenfield construction market, differentiated AI innovations, improved efficiencies, and cross-selling opportunities from the new transaction model,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients. “Key risks include macroeconomic uncertainty that could impact demand.”

The firm, which rates Autodesk shares as Outperform, has a $310 price target on the stock. 

Aside from the upcoming quarterly results, which are set to be released in late May, other bullish catalysts include building the marketplace ecosystem, developing next-gen tools, and “applying AI to drive convergence across design and make processes,” the analysts added.

For Datadog, it's a similar story, as the analysts believe it offers “positive market dynamics, share-gain prospects, a stable revenue model, integral AI positioning, multi-product adoption, and strong free cash flow generation.” The analysts have an Outperform rating and a $200 price target on Datadog.

Upcoming catalysts include its first-quarter results in early May and the company's DASH conference in June.

Zscaler offers product differentiation, competitive positioning, and execution capabilities.

Upcoming catalysts include the company's third-quarter results in late May, as well as its Zenith Live user conference scheduled for June 8–10. “ZS appears well positioned for a beat and raise in line with historical norms, aided in part by recent acquisitions,” the analysts wrote.

data.title: BP's debt boost could dash hopes of return to share buybacks, analyst says
data.description:
BP (BP) down 0.8% in Tuesday's trading after saying it expects an exceptional result in its oil trading business in Q1, with the war in the Middle East fueling market volatility, but also cautioned that volatility will cloud its results and lead to an increase in net debt.

The company said the price environment would result in a $4B-$7B working capital build and an increase in net debt to $25B-$27B at the end of Q1, compared with $22.2B three months earlier.

BP's (BP) increase in net debt could dash hopes of a return of share buybacks after the company halted them earlier this year, AJ Bell analyst Dan Coatsworth said in a note. "That might only be a short-term issue, but it does dampen expectations for BP to share any oil-related windfall with shareholders via buybacks and dividends."

BP's (BP) increase in net debt reflected the need to hold extra working capital to see through a turbulent period in energy markets, Coatsworth said.

The company's oil-trading operations got a boost from market volatility, but BP (BP) will have to wait to benefit from higher prices, the analyst also said.

"BP has given a glimpse into how the Middle East war is impacting its business and it's not all plain sailing,” Coatsworth wrote. "BP doesn't feel the benefit of higher prices immediately as there is a slight delay due to how output is priced on a one- and two-month lagged basis," and any big uptick to production-related earnings will not be felt until Q2.

data.title: Albertsons forecasts $2.22-$2.32 adjusted EPS in fiscal 2026 while raising dividend 13% and resetting $2B buyback
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Albertsons Companies (ACI) Q4 fiscal 2025

Management View

  • Susan Morris (CEO & Director) said the quarter produced “identical sales increased 0.7%” and “better-than-expected adjusted EBITDA of $903 million” despite “greater-than-expected pharmacy headwinds.” She also highlighted capital returns, saying the company “returned more than $1.8 billion to shareholders through share repurchase and dividends.”
  • Morris framed strategy around personalization and footprint, stating, “The future of grocery is personal, and true personalization is a durable competitive advantage,” while also describing network reach: “our stores are within 15 minutes of approximately 120 million people.”
  • Morris detailed structural initiatives, saying the company’s “4 big bets: digital customer experience, merchandising intelligence, labor optimization and supply chain optimization are not pilot programs,” and disclosed a supply-chain tool launch: “we’ve launched Gateway, a proprietary AI-powered tool.”
  • Sharon McCollam (President & CFO) opened with litigation news: “This framework provides for a $774 million settlement payable over 9 years that was recorded during the fourth quarter,” calling it “a meaningful step toward resolving our opioid-related litigation without any admission of wrongdoing or liability.”

Outlook

  • McCollam guided fiscal 2026 identical sales to “0% to 1% or 1.5% to 2.5%, excluding the 150 basis point headwind from the IRA and assuming near flat reported pharmacy sales,” and said “we expect identical sales in the first quarter to track below our full year range, including the IRA and significant ongoing egg deflation,” with “a sequential improvement in sales trends throughout the year.”
  • McCollam set fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA at “$3.85 billion to $3.925 billion” and adjusted EPS at “$2.22 to $2.32,” noting this “including approximately $600 million of share repurchases during fiscal '26.”
  • McCollam guided capital expenditures to “$2 billion to $2.2 billion,” and said the effective tax rate is expected to be “24% to 25%.”
  • Analysts’ estimates were provided in the prompt, but the fiscal-quarter format did not match the required validation rules, so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • McCollam said Q4 identical sales “increased 0.7%,” and explained the variance versus prior expectations: “ID sales in Q4 increased 0.7%, net of approximately 145 basis points of pharmacy-related headwinds versus the expectation we provided in our Q3 outlook of approximately 65 to 70 basis points.”
  • McCollam attributed the pharmacy impact primarily to the Inflation Reduction Act and mix: “IRA pricing and mix pressure accelerated more quickly than expected, while the industry shifted toward a higher generic to brand mix,” which “represented an approximate 105 basis point headwind to ID sales,” adding that GLP-1 moderation “represented an incremental 40 basis point headwind to identical sales compared to our Q3 outlook.”
  • McCollam reported profitability metrics and cost lines, including “Gross margin in Q4 was 27.2%,” “Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 was $903 million,” and “adjusted EPS was $0.48 per diluted share,” while also noting “Q4 interest expense increased $40 million to $141 million.”
  • For the full year, McCollam said “identical sales increased 2%” and “we generated $3.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA,” and detailed fiscal 2025 shareholder returns: “we returned $1.8 billion of capital to shareholders, including $322 million in dividends and nearly $1.5 billion in share repurchases.”

Q&A

  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: Asked for more detail on productivity and COGS vs. SG&A; President & CFO McCollam answered, “We just reset our productivity to $2 billion over the next 3 years,” and CEO Morris added, “The bulk of the savings though will be coming through the SG&A side of the business.”
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: Asked about ex-pharmacy cadence for volumes/inflation; CFO McCollam said, “We’re expecting the industry units to remain pressured, particularly in the first half of the year,” and “expect likely to be positive in the back half.”
  • Mark Carden, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: Asked about competitive pricing investments; CEO Morris said, “We closed the gap on pricing versus MULO in the fourth quarter,” and emphasized, “our approach is very surgical, not broad-based.”
  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: Asked about gross margin trajectory and fuel/freight impacts; CFO McCollam said, “we should see the margin flat to slightly better as we progress through the year in 2026,” adding the outlook includes “pressures that the higher fuel costs will provide related to our transportation and the distribution expenses.”
  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: Asked how buybacks are funded amid higher CapEx and opioid cash needs; CFO McCollam said the company expects “an improvement in working capital,” and “half of that probably will be funded by working capital improvements.”
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: Asked whether better comps would be reinvested or flow to earnings; CFO McCollam said, “we will definitely invest behind the customer and the growth.”
  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: Asked whether higher CapEx is a new baseline and about store openings; CFO McCollam said, “there will be incremental new stores next year,” and advised to think “up 50% from this year,” while adding the company will provide a fiscal 2027 outlook later.
  • Robert Ohmes, BofA Securities, Research Division: Asked if GLP-1 moderation continues and potential traffic impacts; CFO McCollam said the company has “assumed that this GLP-1 pressure will continue” due to payer decisions, and CEO Morris said, “traffic has been fairly steady.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure-testing around unit trends, value perception, margins, and funding of buybacks and CapEx (e.g., questions on “market share erosion,” “flattish margins,” and fuel-driven consumer impacts).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and confident, frequently tying back to productivity/AI and capital returns, including “we feel very confident in the delivery of this new productivity target” (President & CFO McCollam) and “we are entering fiscal '26 from a position of confidence” (CEO Morris).
  • A prior-quarter transcript was not provided, so tone and sentiment changes versus last quarter are not available from the prompt.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous-quarter transcript was not included in the prompt (“not available”), so specific quarter-over-quarter changes in guidance language, KPIs, analyst focus, and tone cannot be directly cited.

Risks and Concerns

  • McCollam identified pharmacy-related top-line pressure, citing “industry-wide pharmacy dynamics” and IRA impacts that “accelerated more quickly than expected,” while also noting the margin effect of generics was “favorable.”
  • McCollam highlighted litigation exposure and resolution steps via the opioid framework: “$774 million settlement payable over 9 years.”
  • Management cited consumer and category pressures, including “units in ID sales in Q4 remained pressured in our lowest income cohorts” (President & CFO McCollam) and “significant ongoing egg deflation” expected to persist into Q1 fiscal 2026.
  • Management acknowledged fuel-cost uncertainty as a potential incremental pressure if it persists, with McCollam noting that if higher costs “continued throughout the year, there could be some incremental pressure.”

Final Takeaway

Management positioned fiscal 2026 as a transition back to earnings growth, pairing a guidance range of $2.22-$2.32 in adjusted EPS and $3.85B-$3.925B in adjusted EBITDA with stepped-up investment ($2B-$2.2B CapEx) and shareholder returns (13% dividend increase, $2B repurchase authorization, and about $600M of repurchases planned in fiscal 2026). The call repeatedly emphasized that pharmacy headwinds from the IRA and GLP-1 dynamics are expected to persist, while productivity, digital/loyalty scaling, and working-capital improvement are intended to support reinvestment, margin stability, and capital return plans.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aura Minerals approves higher capex for Guatemala gold-copper project
data.description:
Aura Minerals (AUGO) up 3% in Tuesday's trading after saying its board approved construction of the Era Dorada gold and copper project in Guatemala and secured budget authorization for an advanced water treatment system.

Aura (AUGO) updated its FY 2026 capital spending guidance to reflect the construction phase, now seeing $262M-$314M, up from a previous capex estimate of $111M-$130M made in February.

The company estimates capex for Era Dorada at $382M, with average gold production estimated at 111K oz/year during the first four years of the project, with an estimated life of mine of 17 years; the project is expected to begin operations in H1 2028.

Including sustaining and exploration capital, Aura (AUGO) said it plans to spend $386M-$453M in FY 2026, up from a previous range of $236M-$278M.

data.title: UPS plans to use RFID package tracking in a major upgrade from manual barcode scans
data.description:
United Parcel Service (UPS) announced on Tuesday that it plans to broadly roll out RFID sensing technology across its integrated network. The company will be the first major logistics provider to deploy the system at that scale. The move is seen as a logistics upgrade that should improve visibility and tracking across shipments, helping the company manage operations more efficiently.

Notably, RFID pickup sensing confirms packages have been picked up and are in UPS’s possession. 

Looking ahead, UPS (UPS) is positioning RFID as a network-wide capability rather than a limited pilot, which could strengthen reliability and data flow in package handling. "We’re lighting up customers’ supply chains in real time with RFID, enabling precise tracking, faster insights, a smarter network, and smarter packages," stated UPS Chief Commercial and Strategy Officer. "This is the most significant visibility advancement in the past decade at UPS and in our industry," he added.
